en.extract:
The African broadbill (Smithornis capensis), also known as the black-capped broadbill or Delacour's broadbill, is a species of bird in the sub-oscine family Calyptomenidae.


Description
The African broadbill is a boldly streaked, largely brown, stocky flycatcher like bird. They have dark crowns which are black in the males and grey in the females of the eastern subspecies and blackish in both sexes of the subspecies S.c. meinertzhageni. The upperparts are mainly olive-brown with black streaks, the bases of the feathers on the lower back and rump are white and are hidden when the bird is at rest. The underparts are buff or creamy-white with black streaks on the flanks and the breast. There is subspecific variation with S.c. meinertzhageni and S.c. suahelicus being whiter below with thicker streaks than, for example S.c. medianus. It measures 12–14 cm (4.7–5.5 in) in length and in South Africa males weighed 21–26.9 g (0.74–0.95 oz) and females 17.4–27.5 g (0.61–0.97 oz).


Sound production
The African broadbill produces a very loud sound which has been compared to an old fashioned car horn. It is loud, vibrating "Prrrrup" which is produced during a display flight, and it may be preceded by softer more plaintive "tui-tui-tui" calls, which are also sometimes made without the car horn call.
Unusually, the call of the African broadbill, like other Smithornis birds, is produced by the vibration of its wings during flight, the sound being produced by the sixth and seventh primary feathers.


Distribution
The African broadbill is native to Africa, especially the southeastern part of the continent. There are two separate allopatric distributions, one rather disjunct distribution in coastal West Africa from Sierra Leone in the west eastwards to the Central African Republic and south to Gabon and the northern Congo. The main distribution covers southern and eastern Africa from Kenya south to KwaZulu Natal.


Habitat
The African broadbill occurs in the middle stratum of evergreen or lowland forest, dense deciduous thickets or other dense woodland, including riverine gallery forest.


Habits
Given its preference for dense forests and woodlands and its inconspicuous behaviour the African broadbill can be difficult to see. It often remains motionless for long periods, perching upright on a low horizontal branch and calling infrequently. It feeds exclusively on invertebrates for which it forages on the ground, in the trees and occasionally hawking them in flight. Beetles, grasshoppers, Hemiptera, caterpillars and spiders have all been recorded as food items.
Both sexes perform a display flight, circling around a perch at a radius of 1bout a metre on rapidly fluttering wings, puffing out the back and rump feathers to show their white feather bases and giving the car horn call. The nest is an oval-shaped structure with a side entrance and is built by both sexes from bark, dry leaves, twigs, grass and rootlets, often held together by strands of spider web. It is suspended conspicuously from a low branch of a tree, usually about 1.5–3 m (4.9–9.8 ft) above the ground. The date the eggs are laid vary from country to country, however it is usually from October–January. The clutch size is 1-3 eggs, which are female takes on sole responsibility for incubating, incubation lasts for 16–17 days, while the male keeps guards the nest, signalling danger with a high-pitched call. The development and care of the young is little know but the male is the main provider of food to the young.


Taxonomy
The generic name honours the Scottish zoologist and explorer Dr Andrew Smith who described this species as Platyrhynchus capensis in 1839.
There are currently nine recognised subspecies and they are listed below with their distributions from north west to south:

Smithornis capensis delacouri Bannerman, 1923 :Sierra Leone, south eastern Guinea and northern Liberia east to Togo.
Smithornis capensis camarunensis Sharpe, 1905: south eastern Nigeria, southern Cameroon, possibly Equatorial Guinea, N Gabon, north western Congo and locally in the south and east of the Central African Republic.
Smithornis capensis albigularis Hartert, 1904: western and northern Angola; south-eastern Democratic Republic of Congo and western and probably central Tanzania south to northern Zambia and northern Malawi.
Smithornis capensis meinertzhageni van Someren, 1919: north eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, Rwanda, Uganda and western Kenya.
Smithornis capensis medianus Hartert & van Someren, 1916: central Kenya and north eastern Tanzania.
Smithornis capensis suahelicus Grote, 1926: south-eastern Kenya, eastern Tanzania and north-eastern Mozambique.
Smithornis capensis conjunctus Clancey, 1963: the Caprivi Strip of north eastern Namibia, northern Botswana, southern Zambia, northern and western Zimbabwe and north western Mozambique.
Smithornis capensis cryptoleucus Clancey, 1963: south-western Tanzania, eastern Zimbabwe, southern Malawi, and central and southern Mozambique south to north eastern Northern Province and north eastern KwaZulu-Natal in South Africa and eastern Eswatini.
Smithornis capensis capensis (A. Smith, 1839) – south eastern KwaZulu-Natal.


Conservation status
This bird has a large range and is common in several regions. In southern Africa it has a scattered distribution because of habitat fragmentation in the area. In general it is considered to be a least-concern species, but it is locally vulnerable due to loss of habitat.
This species lives in forests, dense woodland, and riparian thickets.


References


External links

Videos at the Internet Bird Collection
Recordings at Xeno-canto

The '''African broadbill''' ('''''Smithornis capensis'''''), also known as the '''black-capped broadbill''' or '''Delacour's broadbill''',<ref name = WorldBirdInfo>{{cite web | url = http://worldbirdinfo.net/index.php/birds/details/5093 | title = African Broadbill |access-date= 2016-11-07 | publisher = worldbirdinfo.net | work = World Bird Info | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20161107221650/http://worldbirdinfo.net/index.php/birds/details/5093 | archive-date = 2016-11-07 }}</ref> is a species of [[bird]] in the [[sub-oscine]] family [[Calyptomenidae]].

==Description==
The African broadbill is a boldly streaked, largely brown, stocky flycatcher like bird. They have dark crowns which are black in the males and grey in the females of the eastern [[subspecies]] and blackish in both sexes of the subspecies ''S.c. meinertzhageni''. The upperparts are mainly olive-brown with black streaks, the bases of the feathers on the lower back and rump are white and are hidden when the bird is at rest. The underparts are buff or creamy-white with black streaks on the flanks and the breast. There is subspecific variation with ''S.c. meinertzhageni'' and ''S.c. suahelicus'' being whiter below with thicker streaks than, for example ''S.c. medianus''.<ref name = ZTP>{{cite book | last1 = Zimmerman | first1 = Dale A. | last2 = Turner | first2 = Donald A. | last3 = Pearson | first3 = David J.| year = 1996 | title = Birds of Kenya and Northern Tanzania | publisher = Helm | page = 495 | isbn = 0-7136-3968-7}}</ref> It measures {{Convert|12–14|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length and in [[South Africa]] males weighed {{Convert|21–26.9|g|oz|abbr=on}} and females {{Convert|17.4–27.5|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name = HBW/>

===Sound production===
The African broadbill produces a very loud sound which has been compared to an old fashioned car horn. It is loud, vibrating "Prrrrup" which is produced during a display flight, and it may be preceded by softer more plaintive "tui-tui-tui" calls, which are also sometimes made without the car horn call.<ref name = ZTP/>

Unusually, the call of the African broadbill, like other ''[[Smithornis]]'' birds, is produced by the vibration of its wings during flight, the sound being produced by the sixth and seventh [[primary feathers]].<ref name = Clark>{{Cite journal | author1 = Christopher J. Clark | author2 = Alexander N. G. Kirschel | author3 = Louis Hadjioannou | author4 = Richard O. Prum | year = 2016 | title = ''Smithornis'' broadbills produce loud wingsong by aeroelastic flutter of medial primary wing feathers | doi = 10.1242/jeb.131664 | journal = Journal of Experimental Biology | volume = 19 | issue = 7 | pages =  1069–1075 | pmid = 27030781 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

==Distribution==
The African broadbill is native to [[Africa]], especially the southeastern part of the continent.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> There are two separate [[allopatric]] distributions,<ref name = Iziko>{{cite web | url = http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/smithornis_capensis.htm | title = ''Smithornis capensis'' (African broadbill) |access-date= 2016-11-07 | publisher = Iziko Museums of South Africa|work = Biodiversity Explorer}}</ref> one rather disjunct distribution in coastal West Africa from [[Sierra Leone]] in the west eastwards to the [[Central African Republic]] and south to [[Gabon]] and the northern [[Republic of Congo|Congo]]. The main distribution covers southern and eastern Africa from [[Kenya]] south to [[KwaZulu Natal]].<ref name = HBW>{{cite web | url = http://www.hbw.com/species/african-broadbill-smithornis-capensis | title = African Broadbill (''Smithornis capensis'') |access-date= 2016-11-07 | publisher = Lynx Edicions|work = Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive}}</ref>

==Habitat==
The African broadbill occurs in the middle stratum of evergreen or lowland forest, dense deciduous thickets or other dense woodland,<ref name=sab>{{cite web| url = http://sabap2.adu.org.za/docs/sabap1/490.pdf | title = African Broadbill |access-date= 2016-11-07 | publisher = Animal Demography Unit|work = SABAP - Southern African Bird Atlas Project}}</ref> including riverine gallery forest.<ref name = ZTP/>

==Habits==
Given its preference for dense forests and woodlands and its inconspicuous behaviour the African broadbill can be difficult to see. It often remains motionless for long periods, perching upright on a low horizontal branch and calling infrequently.<ref name = ZTP/><ref name = Iziko/> It feeds exclusively on invertebrates for which it forages on the ground, in the trees and occasionally hawking them in flight. [[Beetle]]s, [[grasshopper]]s, [[Hemiptera]], [[caterpillar]]s and [[spider]]s have all been recorded as food items.<ref name = Iziko/>

Both sexes perform a display flight, circling around a perch at a radius of 1bout a metre on rapidly fluttering wings, puffing out the back and rump feathers to show their white feather bases and giving the car horn call.<ref name = ZTP/> The nest is an oval-shaped structure with a side entrance and is built by both sexes from bark, dry leaves, twigs, grass and rootlets, often held together by strands of spider web. It is suspended conspicuously from a low branch of a tree, usually about {{Convert|1.5–3|m|ft|abbr=on}} above the ground. The date the eggs are laid vary from country to country, however it is usually from October–January. The clutch size is 1-3 eggs, which are female takes on sole responsibility for incubating, incubation lasts for 16–17 days, while the male keeps guards the nest, signalling danger with a high-pitched call. The development and care of the young is little know but the male is the main provider of food to the young.<ref name = Iziko/>

==Taxonomy==
The generic name honours the [[Scotland|Scottish]] zoologist and explorer [[Andrew Smith (zoologist)|Dr Andrew Smith]] who [[species description|described]] this species as ''Platyrhynchus capensis'' in 1839.<ref name = Jobling/>

There are currently nine recognised subspecies<ref name = ITIS/> and they are listed below with their distributions from north west to south:<ref name = ITIS /><ref name = HBW/>

*''Smithornis capensis delacouri'' <small>Bannerman, 1923</small> :Sierra Leone, south eastern [[Guinea]] and northern [[Liberia]] east to [[Togo]]. 
*''Smithornis capensis camarunensis'' <small>Sharpe, 1905</small>: south eastern [[Nigeria]], southern [[Cameroon]], possibly [[Equatorial Guinea]], N Gabon, north western Congo and locally in the south and east of the Central African Republic. 
*''Smithornis capensis albigularis'' <small>Hartert, 1904</small>: western and northern [[Angola]]; south-eastern [[Democratic Republic of Congo]] and western and probably central [[Tanzania]] south to northern [[Zambia]] and northern [[Malawi]]. 
*''Smithornis capensis meinertzhageni'' <small>van Someren, 1919</small>: north eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, [[Rwanda]], [[Uganda]] and western Kenya. 
*''Smithornis capensis medianus'' <small>Hartert & van Someren, 1916</small>: central Kenya and north eastern Tanzania. 
*''Smithornis capensis suahelicus'' <small>Grote, 1926</small>: south-eastern Kenya, eastern Tanzania and north-eastern [[Mozambique]]. 
*''Smithornis capensis conjunctus'' <small>Clancey, 1963</small>: the [[Caprivi Strip]] of north eastern [[Namibia]], northern [[Botswana]], southern Zambia, northern and western [[Zimbabwe]] and north western [[Mozambique]].
*''Smithornis capensis cryptoleucus'' <small>Clancey, 1963</small>: south-western Tanzania, eastern Zimbabwe, southern Malawi, and central and southern Mozambique south to north eastern [[Limpopo|Northern Province]] and north eastern KwaZulu-Natal in South Africa and eastern [[Eswatini]]. 
*''Smithornis capensis capensis'' <small>(A. Smith, 1839)</small> – south eastern KwaZulu-Natal.

==Conservation status==
This bird has a large range and is common in several regions.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> In southern Africa it has a scattered distribution because of [[habitat fragmentation]] in the area.<ref name=sab/> In general it is considered to be a [[least-concern species]],<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> but it is locally vulnerable due to loss of habitat.<ref name=sab/>

This species lives in forests, dense woodland, and [[river|riparian]] thickets.<ref name=sab/>

zh.extract:
非洲阔嘴鸟（学名：Smithornis capensis），是阔嘴鸟科非洲阔嘴鸟属的一种，分布于津巴布韦、加蓬、斯威士兰、几内亚比绍、赞比亚、科特迪瓦、安哥拉、南非、卢旺达、喀麦隆、纳米比亚、加纳、中非共和国、尼日利亚、利比里亚、肯尼亚、坦桑尼亚、刚果民主共和国、博茨瓦纳、刚果、马拉维、塞拉利昂、乌干达和莫桑比克。全球活动范围约为3,010,000平方千米。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
其种加词“capensis”意为“南非开普省的/好望角的”。
非洲阔嘴鸟的平均体重约为23.2克。栖息地包括亚热带或热带的（低地）湿润疏灌丛、亚热带或热带的（低地）干燥疏灌丛、亚热带或热带的湿润低地林、亚热带或热带的旱林、耕地、种植园、干燥的稀树草原和亚热带或热带的湿润山地林。
